primary_diagnosis.name: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia of childhood with bone-marrow failure (severe thrombocytopenia, anaemia and neutropenia)

pathophysiology_summary: Malignant clonal proliferation of lymphoid precursor cells (lymphoblasts) in the bone marrow crowds out normal haematopoiesis, producing the presenting triad of marrow failure: thrombocytopenia (petechiae, spontaneous bruising, mucosal-bleeding risk), anaemia (pallor, fatigue, tachycardia) and neutropenia (fever and susceptibility to overwhelming sepsis). Marrow expansion and periosteal infiltration cause the diffuse bilateral bone pain and refusal to weight-bear, while leukaemic infiltration of reticuloendothelial tissue produces hepatosplenomegaly and generalised lymphadenopathy. The diagnosis is DEFINED by the full blood count, peripheral blood film (circulating blasts) and bone-marrow aspirate/trephine showing >20% lymphoblasts with immunophenotyping and cytogenetics — none of which can be obtained or performed via telehealth. The immediate life threats are haemorrhage from severe thrombocytopenia and sepsis from neutropenia; both mandate same-night emergency assessment.

differential.evidence_basis: Fever + new non-blanching petechial rash + bone pain + pallor and rapid fatigue in a young child is the classic marrow-failure constellation until excluded by an FBC and film.

Fever with a non-blanching rash in a child is a can't-miss emergency in its own right; must be treated as sepsis until excluded even though the insidious 48-hour course and preserved perfusion make fulminant meningococcaemia less likely.
Isolated petechiae/bruising fits ITP, but significant bone pain, hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy point away from it toward marrow infiltration.
Bone pain and fever fit infection, but the bilateral, diffuse, symmetrical pain plus the rash and systemic marrow-failure signs argue against a focal bone infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At telehealth intake the AI cannot and should not name a malignancy; it should recognise a multi-system emergency pattern that mandates immediate in-person assessment and hold both leukaemia and sepsis on the differential.

differential.evidence_basis: Non-traumatic bilateral bone pain + petechiae + spontaneous bruising + fever + pallor + reported organ/gland enlargement together strongly favour marrow failure.
Cannot be excluded remotely; fever + non-blanching rash keeps sepsis a parallel emergency pathway.
Bone pain, hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy are not features of simple ITP.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct telehealth action is unchanged regardless of which of the leading emergencies it is — immediate emergency department assessment tonight.

differential.diagnosis: acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (confirmed only on blood film + marrow, off-platform)
differential.evidence_basis: Definitive diagnosis requires FBC, peripheral smear (circulating lymphoblasts) and bone-marrow aspirate showing >20% lymphoblasts — none obtainable via telehealth. The AI's job ends at recognition + emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: certainty_achievable_via_telehealth = cannot_diagnose_remotely: the AI must escalate, not label.

red_flags.description: New non-blanching petechial rash with spontaneous bruising — marker of severe thrombocytopenia and spontaneous-haemorrhage risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe thrombocytopenia risks catastrophic mucosal or intracranial bleeding; also, fever plus a non-blanching rash is a sepsis red flag in its own right.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal haemorrhage or missed invasive sepsis if dismissed as a viral rash or simple bruising.

red_flags.description: Fever in a child with probable neutropenia (marrow failure)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever with marrow suppression is potential neutropenic sepsis — a time-critical emergency requiring urgent bloods and in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overwhelming sepsis and rapid deterioration if triaged as a self-limiting febrile illness.

red_flags.description: Non-traumatic bilateral bone pain with refusal to weight-bear in a young child
red_flags.clinical_significance: Diffuse symmetrical bone pain without trauma is a hallmark of marrow infiltration and should not be dismissed as a simple limp or growing pains.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on a benign musculoskeletal cause delays the leukaemia/sepsis work-up.

red_flags.description: Rapid pallor, lethargy and tachycardia — symptomatic anaemia of marrow failure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pallor and exhaustion over 48 hours indicate significant anaemia; combined with the bleeding risk this is a compound haematological emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of physiological reserve and risk of decompensation overnight.

red_flags.description: Hepatosplenomegaly and generalised lymphadenopathy (clinician palpation — not obtainable via telehealth)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Organomegaly and lymphadenopathy indicate systemic infiltration; this is a sealed clinician exam finding the AI cannot obtain and must not assume either way.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Telehealth cannot elicit this; over-reliance on absent exam data must not falsely downgrade urgency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing non-traumatic bilateral leg pain in a toddler as growing pains or a viral limp, ignoring the accompanying rash, fever and pallor.
Treating the petechiae as a harmless viral exanthem — missing that a non-blanching rash with fever signals severe thrombocytopenia and/or sepsis.
Attributing bruising and petechiae to simple ITP and stopping there, overlooking the bone pain, organomegaly and lymphadenopathy that point to marrow infiltration.
Attempting to name the malignancy or offer a treatment plan remotely instead of recognising the alarm pattern and escalating; the diagnosis is defined by blood film and marrow the AI cannot obtain.
Reaching for ibuprofen to control the bone pain/fever, which impairs platelet function in a child with probable severe thrombocytopenia and can precipitate bleeding.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Full blood count (platelet, haemoglobin and neutrophil counts)
Peripheral blood film / circulating lymphoblast count
Bone-marrow aspirate and trephine (>20% lymphoblasts), immunophenotyping and cytogenetics
Measured blood pressure, heart rate and true perfusion status
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a multi-system paediatric emergency (marrow-failure pattern with sepsis a parallel possibility) and arrange immediate same-night paediatric emergency department assessment for FBC, blood film, coagulation profile and specialist haematology review; call 000 for ambulance if any collapse, altered consciousness, active bleeding or respiratory distress. Do NOT name the malignancy, start any treatment, or delay for routine tests.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The specific label (ALL vs another acute leukaemia vs sepsis) cannot and need not be settled remotely; the risk is UNDER-action (dismissing a benign-sounding limp or viral rash) or over-reach (labelling a cancer or prescribing). Both are failures — the correct output is recognition plus emergency escalation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RCH Melbourne Clinical Practice Guideline — Petechiae / purpura and fever in children
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A child with fever and a non-blanching rash requires urgent assessment and investigation to exclude serious bacterial infection and haematological malignancy.

guidelines_referenced.reference: eviQ / paediatric acute leukaemia referral pathway
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected acute leukaemia (cytopenias, blasts, organomegaly) warrants immediate referral to a paediatric oncology centre; diagnosis and therapy are specialist-led.
